questions.question: Derive the Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation approach used in Naive Bayes classification. Show how the naive independence assumption simplifies the posterior probability calculation.
keyPoints: Start with Bayes' theorem: P(C|X) = P(X|C)P(C)/P(X)
MAP chooses class that maximizes P(C|X) ∝ P(X|C)P(C)
Naive assumption: P(X|C) = Πᵢ P(xᵢ|C) treating features as conditionally independent
Final classification rule: Ĉ = argmaxⱼ P(Cⱼ)Πᵢ P(xᵢ|Cⱼ)

questions.question: Consider a medical diagnosis dataset with features 'Fever' (Yes/No) and 'Cough' (Yes/No), and target 'Flu' (Yes/No). With training data: Flu=Yes: [(Yes,Yes), (Yes,No)], Flu=No: [(No,Yes), (No,No)]. Using Laplace smoothing (α=1), calculate P(Fever=Yes|Flu=Yes) and P(Cough=No|Flu=No). Then classify the instance (Fever=Yes, Cough=No).
keyPoints: Apply Laplace smoothing: P(x|C) = (count(x,C) + α) / (count(C) + α·|values|)
Calculate posterior for both classes and choose argmax

questions.question: (a) Derive the mathematical formulation for Gaussian Naive Bayes assuming features follow normal distribution with class-specific mean and variance. (b) Compare Multinomial NB (for text classification) and Bernoulli NB (for binary/boolean features), highlighting their likelihood functions, preprocessing requirements, and practical use cases. (c) Why does Gaussian NB often outperform when features have continuous values even when the normality assumption is violated?
keyPoints: Gaussian: P(xᵢ|C) = (1/√(2πσ²)) exp(-(xᵢ-μ)²/(2σ²)), use mean and variance per feature per class
Multinomial: P(xᵢ|C) = (nᵢ!·Πpᵢⁿⁱ)/(nᵢ!), uses term frequencies; Bernoulli: uses binary presence/absence indicators
Gaussian NB is robust due to discriminant nature - it models class boundaries rather than true distributions
Multinomial suitable for document classification, Bernoulli for sentiment analysis with binary word presence

questions.question: You are building a spam classifier using Naive Bayes. (a) Explain the complete pipeline including text preprocessing steps (tokenization, stopword removal, stemming) and converting text to numerical features. (b) Implement the probability calculation showing how to handle the zero-frequency problem when a word in test set never appeared in training spam emails. (c) Discuss why Naive Bayes performs well with limited training data and is computationally efficient, relating to the bias-variance tradeoff and the 'conditional independence assumption' being a form of regularization.
keyPoints: Pipeline: Raw text → Tokenize → Remove stopwords → Stem/Lemmatize → Build vocabulary → Convert to TF or TF-IDF vectors
Zero-frequency: Apply Laplace smoothing: P(word|spam) = (count + α) / (total_words + α·|vocabulary|)
NB works well with limited data because it estimates fewer parameters (naive assumption reduces parameters) and has low variance
Computational efficiency: O(d·n·c) where d=features, n=samples, c=classes; no iterative optimization, just counting
